summary: DeVault reports the apparent cancellation of Jack Dorsey's FOSDEM '25 keynote following community protest, while pivoting to deeper questions about FOSDEM's governance and transparency. He thanks FOSDEM staff but notes the lack of official communication and the troubling revelation that 3 of 4 independent reviewers rejected Dorsey's talk proposal yet it was accepted anyway. The post calls for using the now-empty time slot to discuss what transparent, participatory governance at FOSDEM should look like. A later notice reveals the discussion session was also cancelled after organizers agreed that de-platforming Dorsey was sufficient victory, with governance reform efforts to continue after the event.
key_points: Dorsey's FOSDEM keynote was apparently cancelled after community protest, though no official statement was made

The controversy exposed deeper governance problems: talk selection overrode 3 of 4 reviewer rejections, and FOSDEM has no publicly documented processes for talk selection, conflict of interest, sponsorship terms, or decision-making
Multiple community members including long-time speakers and devroom organizers have raised transparency concerns about FOSDEM's budget, legal structure, and organizational governance
The protest organizers declared de-platforming Dorsey as mission success and chose not to improvise further action at the event itself
Reform efforts will continue after FOSDEM, shifting from protest to institutional advocacy for transparent governance
money_quotes: This year is FOSDEM's 25th anniversary, and it seems sorely overdue for graduating from the "trust us, it's crazy behind the scenes" governance model to something more aligned with the spirit of open source.
It strains our presumption of good faith when the talk proposal was rejected by 3 of the 4 independent reviewers and went through anyway.

It's kind of weird that we have to take them at their word — that the talk selection process isn't documented anywhere publicly, nor the conflict of interest policy, nor the sponsorship terms, nor almost anything at all about how FOSDEM operates or is governed internally.
Who makes decisions? How? We don't know, and that's kind of weird for something so important in the open source space.
We trust the FOSDEM organizers — we can extend them the benefit of the doubt when they tell us that talk selection is independent of sponsorships.
key_insight: The Dorsey keynote controversy at FOSDEM revealed that one of open source's most important institutions lacks the very transparency and participatory governance that the movement itself champions.
